mnemonics: C.A.S.H. M.A.P.S: Cost, Associativity, Size, Hit time - Mapping, Access, Performance, Size
Direct, Set, Full: Don't Set Fire to memory access!
keyPoints: Cache mapping determines how a main memory address is placed into the cache. The three primary methods are Direct Mapped, Fully Associative, and Set Associative.
The 'cost' of a memory system includes the physical price of the chips and the performance cost of access time (latency) and miss rate.
A larger cache size generally decreases the miss rate but increases the cost, access time (hit time), and power consumption.
commonMistakes: Confusing block size (line size) with cache size. Cache size is total capacity; block size is the amount of data brought in on a miss.
Assuming a direct-mapped cache has only one block total. It has one block per set, and the number of sets determines its overall size.

Thinking that a fully associative cache has the fastest access time. While it has the lowest conflict miss rate, its hardware complexity (searching all lines) makes it slower than a direct-mapped cache for a hit.
